elements.sent-2411-001: Observations have revealed unique temperature profiles in hot Jupiter atmospheres.
elements.sent-2411-002: We propose that the energy transport by vertical mixing could lead to such thermal features.
elements.sent-2411-003: In our new scenario, strong absorbers, TiO and VO are not necessary.
elements.sent-2411-004: Vertical mixing could be naturally excited by atmospheric circulation or internal gravity wave breaking.
elements.sent-2411-005: We perform radiative transfer calculations by taking into account the vertical mixing driven energy transport.
elements.sent-2411-006: The radiative equilibrium (RE) is replaced by radiative-mixing equilibrium (RME).
elements.sent-2411-007: We investigate how the mixing strength, $K_{\rm zz}$, affects the atmospheric temperature-pressure profile.
elements.sent-2411-008: Strong mixing can heat the lower atmosphere and cool the upper atmosphere.
elements.sent-2411-009: This effect has important effects on the atmosphere thermal features that would form without mixing.
elements.sent-2411-010: In certain circumstances, it can induce temperature inversions in scenarios where the temperature monotonically increases with increasing pressure under conditions of lower thermal band opacity.
elements.sent-2411-011: Temperature inversions show up as $K_{\rm zz}$ increases with altitude due to shear interaction with the convection layer.
elements.sent-2411-012: The atmospheric thermal structure of HD~209458b can be well fitted with $K_{\rm zz \propto (P/1\ {\rm bar )^{-1/2}\ {\rm cm ^{2 \ {\rm s ^{-1}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Our findings suggest vertical mixing promotes temperature inversions and lowers $K_{\rm zz}$ estimates compared to prior studies.
elements.sent-2411-014: Incorporating chemical species into vertical mixing will significantly affect the thermal profile due to their temperature sensitivity.
elements.sent-2411-015: Hot Jupiters orbit close to the host star and receive tremendous radiation from their parent star \citep{2007prpl...Charbonneau .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Due to the vicinity to their host star, they usually become tidally locked \citep{1996ApJ..Guillot .}
elements.sent-2411-017: This results in a significant temperature disparity between the day and night sides.
elements.sent-2411-018: Hot Jupiters constitute a distinct category among more than 5,700 known exoplanets, characterized by their expanded radii (e.g., \citealt{2002A A...Guillot,2004ApJ...Gu,2010ApJ...Batygin,2010ApJ...Youdin,2013ApJ...Spiegel,2017ApJ...Komacek,2022MNRAS...Hou ) and diverse infrared emission properties, some of which lead to stratospheric thermal inversions (\citealt{2003ApJ...Hubeny ; \citealt{2005ApJ...Fortney ; \citealt{2010A A...Guillot , hereafter G\citeyear{2010A A...Guillot ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Notably, the Spitzer Space Telescope detected this feature in the spectrum of the hot Jupiter HD~209458b \citep{2008ApJ...Knutson .}
elements.sent-2411-020: In hot Jupiters, primary chemicals like Titanium oxide (TiO) and Vanadium oxide (VO) \citep{2003ApJ...Hubeny,2005ApJ...Fortney absorb ultraviolet (UV)/optical light in the upper atmosphere, causing localized heating and temperature inversions.}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, in cooler hot Jupiters like HD~209458b, these chemicals would condense deeper and be hard to detect, making temperature inversions unlikely \citep{2016ApJ...Line,2024MNRAS.Roth .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Specifically, HD 209458b is currently not considered to have a temperature inversion \citep{2014ApJ...Diamond-Lowe,2016AJ....152..Line .}
elements.sent-2411-023: While many other planets, particularly ultra-hot Jupiters, exhibit clear temperature inversions, HD 209458b does not fall into this category.
elements.sent-2411-024: Vertical mixing plays a crucial role in tracking the movement of chemicals and clouds \citep{2016AA...Fromang,2018MNRAS...Ryu,2018ApJ...Zhang....1Z,2018ApJ...Zhang,2019MNRAS...Menou,2019A A...Ormel , and contributes significantly to chemical disequilibrium \citep{2007ApJ...669.Hubeny,2022MNRAS.511.Tan,2023MNRAS.523.Lee in the atmosphere.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Effective vertical mixing prevents the appearance of cold traps \citep{2009ApJ..Spiegel,2013A A...Parmentier,2023Natur.Pelletier,2024arXivZhang , and promotes both cloud formation and transport of materials from deeper atmospheric layers to higher altitudes \citep{2024arXivSing .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Recent studies have determined the vertical mixing strength in the atmosphere of WASP-107b through spectroscopic observations and atmospheric retrievals.
elements.sent-2411-027: These studies highlight the varying strengths and effects of vertical mixing between different models, making an accurate estimation of the vertical mixing strength crucial.
elements.sent-2411-028: This work focuses on the energy transport of vertical mixing in a radiative atmosphere, contrasting with previous research that emphasized mass transport by turbulence and large-scale convection \citep{Zhang_2020 , and the interaction between horizontal mixing and radiative transfer (G\citeyear{2010A A...Guillot ).}}
elements.sent-2411-029: In low-density upper atmospheres, such as Earth's mesosphere, breaking gravity waves significantly mix chemical tracers \citep{1987JGR....92.6691S, Zhang_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: In atmospheres with stable stratification, vertical mixing is driven by large-scale overturning and wave mixing \citep{1984maph...Holton, Zhang_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: However, the role of vertical mixing energy in the radiative layer, especially in conjunction with radiative transfer, remains unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-032: Investigating this energy transport is essential.
elements.sent-2411-033: We study the influences of vertical mixing, driven by atmospheric circulation \citep{Zhang_2020 or breaking gravity waves \citep{1987JGR....92.6691S, Zhang_2020 , on the temperature-pressure profiles of exoplanet atmospheres.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Actually, the energy transport induced by the vertical mixing provides an alternative explanation for the thermal inversion.
elements.sent-2411-035: This differs from the mechanisms proposed by \cite{2003ApJ...Hubeny,2008ApJ...Fortney,2018ApJ...Lothringer,2019MNRAS...Gandhi , which attribute strong inversions to the presence of visible absorbers (TiO, VO, Fe, AlO, CaO, NaH, and MgH) with reasonable abundance or low infrared opacity due to the low abundance of ${\rm H_2O}$.}
elements.sent-2411-036: This study utilizes the mechanical greenhouse effect schematic as presented in \cite{2010ApJ...Youdin .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Unlike convection, which generates an outward energy flux, forced turbulence in stable regions induces a downward energy flux.
elements.sent-2411-038: This flux introduces changes to the planet's radiative equilibrium (RE), resulting in a radiative-mixing equilibrium (RME).
elements.sent-2411-039: This downward heat flux can compensate for or even exceed the convective losses.
elements.sent-2411-040: The vertical mixing intensity $K_{\rm zz}$ will be treated as a free parameter.
elements.sent-2411-041: An increase in $K_{\rm zz}$ leads to stronger vertical mixing.
elements.sent-2411-042: Furthermore, the temperature variations are controlled by changes in the gradient of the mixing flux.
elements.sent-2411-043: As the depth increases, the gradient of the mixing flux first intensifies and then diminishes, resulting in the development of a temperature inversion.
elements.sent-2411-044: Since vertical mixing could heat the lower atmosphere, we can estimate the mixing intensity by matching the temperature-pressure profiles with those retrieved by transmission observations.
elements.sent-2411-045: This work is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-046: The RME driven by vertical mixing is shown in \S \Ref{sec:atmosphere .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Additionally, we examine the impact of vertical mixing on the structure of the semi-grey atmosphere and verify its consistency with the atmospheric simulation results of \cite{2008ApJ..Showman in \S \Ref{sec_reslut .}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Finally, conclusions and discussions are presented in \S \ref{sec_conclusion .}
elements.sent-2411-049: A planetary atmosphere that is affected by external radiation, such as that from a star, is referred to as an irradiated atmosphere.
elements.sent-2411-050: Atmospheric circulation or breaking gravity waves can create vertical mixing and force downward flux, similar to how a greenhouse works, possibly changing the temperature structures in planetary \citep{2010ApJ...Youdin .}
elements.sent-2411-051: We here set up the theoretical framework used to examine how vertical mixing can affect semi-grey atmospheres.
elements.sent-2411-052: We summarize the equations related to radiative equilibrium in \S \ref{sec:Radiative_transfer , and the equations related to RME in \S \ref{sec:RTE .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: By solving these equations, the effects of the vertical mixing on the exoplanetary atmosphere could be investigated.
elements.sent-2411-057: The optical depth relationship is given by $d\tau_{\nu = - \kappa_{\nu dz$ with altitude $z$.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Additionally, external stellar irradiation, denoted as $H_{\rm ext}$, can be calculated from the incoming flux $F_{\rm ext}$ using $H_{\rm ext = F_{\rm ext /4\pi$.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The RTE tends to diverge and must be regulated using Eq.(\ref{eq:conservation_case01 ).}
elements.sent-2411-064: The critical threshold is established at a pressure of $P_{\rm crit = 1\, \text{bar}$.}
elements.sent-2411-065: When the pressure is below $P_{\rm crit}$, the coefficients $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are assigned values of 0 and 1, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-066: In contrast, when pressure exceeds $P_{\rm crit}$, the coefficients $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are set to 1 and 0, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-067: This method effectively reduces numerical instabilities caused by high opacity.
elements.sent-2411-068: Under typical circumstances and in the absence of external influences, heat is transferred from warmer to cooler areas in accordance with the second law of thermodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-069: Nevertheless, close to the IR photosphere, vertical mixing can occur due to atmospheric circulation or the breaking of gravity waves.
elements.sent-2411-070: This phenomenon enables heat to move from cooler to warmer regions, thus disrupting the conventional heat transfer routes and modifying the energy balance of the system.
elements.sent-2411-071: Since the sound wave travels faster than the heat diffuses in the fluid parcel, it conserves entropy $S$ and maintains the equilibrium of pressure $P$ with its surroundings as it moves radially over a distance $\ell$.
elements.sent-2411-072: The temperature disparity between the blob and its environment is \begin{equation}\delta T = \left(\left.\frac{dT}{dz}\right|_{\mathrm{ad}} - \frac{dT}{dz}\right) \ell = - \ell \frac{ T}{c_{\rm p} } \frac{dS}{dz} \ ,\end{equation} with isobaric heat capacity $c_{\rm p}$.
elements.sent-2411-073: In a stably stratified region, the entropy gradient is given by $dS/dz = g/T\left(1-\nabla/\nabla_{\rm ad}\right)>0$.
elements.sent-2411-074: The descending particles have the potential to warm the atmosphere.
elements.sent-2411-075: The extra heat $\delta q = \rho c_{\rm p \delta T$.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Thus, the heat flux by vertical mixing flux is given by \begin{equation}F_{\text{mix}} = w \delta q = -K_{\rm zz} \rho g \left(1 - \frac{\nabla}{\nabla_{\mathrm{ad}}}\right) \ , \label{eq_f_mix}\end{equation} with the eddy speed $w$, the mixing diffusion $ K_{\rm zz = w \ell $, and gravitational acceleration $g$.}

elements.sent-2411-084: The Rybicki scheme starts with the same set of linearized structural equations, reorganizing the state vector and the Jacobi matrix.

elements.sent-2411-092: Here we describe how we incorporate the mixing induced energy transport into the calculation of the atmosphere temperature profile.
elements.sent-2411-093: In a semi-grey atmosphere, where opacity arises from both visible and thermal infrared bands, the energy equation corresponding to Eq.(\ref{eq_rme1 ) at the upper atmosphere simplifies to \begin{equation}\kappa_{\rm th} \left(J_{\rm th} - B\right) + \kappa_{\rm v}J_{\rm v} +\frac{ g}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{d} F_{\text {mix}}}{\mathrm{d} P}=0\ .\end{equation}.}

elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{modified_response_02.pdf} \caption{The left panel shows the effect of varying mixing strengths $K_{\rm zz}$ on the semi-grey atmosphere for $\mu = 0.02$ as $\nabla_{\rm ad}=2/7$, whereas the right panel depicts the scenario for $\mu = 0.5$. The small panels within the left and right panels are zoomed-in views of local areas. The colors red, blue, orange, and green correspond to increasing $K_{\rm zz}$. The black dots represent the atmospheric structure obtained from Equation (27) in G2010. The onset of the pseudo-adiabatic zone's formation is denoted by the circular dot. The dashed lines denote the pseudo-adiabatic regions. Additionally, $g = 1000 \ \rm cm \ s^{-2}$, $T_{\rm int} = 100\ K$, and $T_{\rm irr} = 1469\ K$. } \label{fig1_modifies}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{modified_response_01.pdf} \caption{The left panel shows the effect of varying mixing strengths $K_{\rm zz}$ on the semi-grey atmosphere for $\mu = 0.02$ as $\nabla_{\rm ad}=1/7$, whereas the right panel depicts the scenario for $\mu = 0.5$. The mixing strength $K_{zz}$ ranges from 0 to $10^9\ cm^2/s$, represented by different colors. The triangular markers denote the boundaries of the pseudo-adiabatic region, and the circular markers indicate the radiation-convection boundaries (RCB). The dashed lines denote the pseudo-adiabatic regions, while the thick solid lines indicate the convectively unstable region. All other parameters are consistent with Figure \ref{fig1_modifies}. } \label{fig1_modified_02}

elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{varing_kzz_03.pdf} \caption{ The temperature-pressure profiles in the atmosphere vary with the mixing coefficient $ K_{\rm zz}$ scaling as $ K_{\rm zz} \propto \left(P/ 1\ bar\right)^{\zeta} $. The dotted line represents the scenario without mixing. Dotted and dashed lines indicate increased (positive $\zeta$) and decreased (negative $\zeta$) mixing strengths, respectively. The colors red, green, orange, and royal blue correspond to $\left|\zeta\right|$ values of 0.6, 0.5, 0.25, and 0.1. Additionally, the purple line illustrates the scenario with a constant $ K_{\rm zz} $. } \label{fig3}

The red line in the left panel shows $\zeta = 0.6$, while the right panel shows $\zeta = -0.25$. } \label{fig4_energy_flux}

elements.sent-2411-257: We incorporate the vertical mixing into a radiative equilibrium atmosphere to establish a radiative-mixing equilibrium atmosphere.
elements.sent-2411-258: In this work, we examine whether the vertical mixing can enhance the development of {\bf a temperature inversion in the planet atmosphere.}
elements.sent-2411-259: It also estimates the vertical mixing strength based on the atmospheric structure of HD~209458b, as suggested by \cite{2008ApJ..Showman .}
elements.sent-2411-260: Vertical mixing significantly influences temperature profiles, where $\mu$, $\gamma$, and $K_{\rm zz}$ collectively dictate its effects.
elements.sent-2411-261: In conditions where $\gamma > 1$ and $\mu < 0.5$, temperature inversions naturally occur in the absence of mixing, but these are mitigated by robust, continuous mixing.
elements.sent-2411-262: Conversely, when $\mu \ge 0.5$, temperature profiles generally exhibit a steady increase with increasing pressure for the cases without mixing.
elements.sent-2411-263: Vertical mixing raises temperatures at greater depths, reducing temperature inversions as $\mu < 0.5$ and promoting a gradual temperature increase as $\mu \ge 0.5$.
elements.sent-2411-264: It also creates pseudo-adiabatic regions, shifting the radiative-convective boundary (RCB) inward.
elements.sent-2411-265: A minor temperature inversion is induced by vertical mixing, as the temperature profiles with increasing pressure are established with reduced thermal opacity.
elements.sent-2411-266: Spatial variations in $K_{\rm zz}$ can dynamically intensify temperature inversions or heat deeper layers in certain circumstances.
elements.sent-2411-267: For HD 209458b, a diffusion profile $K_{\rm zz \propto (P/1\ \text{bar )^{-1/2 \ \text{cm ^2 \ \text{s ^{-1}$ is applied, indicating a top mixing strength of approximately $10^{7 -10^{8.3 \ \text{cm ^2 \ \text{s ^{-1}$, consistent with its deep atmospheric structure.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-268: This study emphasizes the critical role of vertical mixing in shaping temperature profiles, highlighting a decrease in mixing strength at the top atmosphere compared to previous findings.
elements.sent-2411-269: The current RME model has shown promise in practical applications, but it has some limitations and prospects.
elements.sent-2411-270: These limitations arise primarily from the lack of consideration of the actual convective zone and chemical equilibrium in non-grey atmospheres.
elements.sent-2411-271: Firstly, by incorporating the actual convective zone, we can achieve a more precise evaluation of the temperature gradients of the planet \citep{2012ApJ...Robinson,2015A A...Parmentier,2023ApJ...Zhang .}
elements.sent-2411-272: Secondly, chemical elements play a crucial role in planetary atmospheres as they not only influence the atmospheric climate \citep{2010.book..Pierrehumbert , but also modify the structure of the atmosphere through interactions with clouds \citep{2017MNRAS...Hubeny .}}
elements.sent-2411-273: Chemical elements are very sensitive to temperature changes, with even small fluctuations having a large impact.
elements.sent-2411-274: We have shown that vertical mixing significantly affects temperature, suggesting that chemical elements will shift noticeably.
elements.sent-2411-275: In addition, the RME under general conditions could affect a planet's runaway greenhouse effect \citep{2023Natur.Selsis by altering the levels of water vapor and the allocation of carbon and hydrogen within the magma ocean.}
elements.sent-2411-276: Our atmosphere model is based on the Gensis model \citep{2017MNRAS...GENESIS,Piette...phdthesis , which can effectively incorporate considerations of chemical equilibrium in multi-band frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-277: These enhancements will provide us with better insight into exoplanet habitability, climate, transition radius \citep{2008ApJS..Hansen , observational characteristics (transmission spectrum, \citealt{2022AJ....Zhou ), while having significant implications for explaining planetary mass and water content within the TRAPPIST-1 system.}}
elements.sent-2411-278: Furthermore, both isotropic and nonisotropic scattering \citep{2014ApJS..215...Heng can also alter the temperature-pressure profile of the atmosphere.}
elements.sent-2411-279: Furthermore, RME can shift the position of the photosphere, significantly influencing the mass loss of the envelope through photoevaporation \citep{2023ApJ...Modirrousta .}

elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{modified_response_02.pdf} \caption{The left panel shows the effect of varying mixing strengths $K_{\rm zz}$ on the semi-grey atmosphere for $\mu = 0.02$ as $\nabla_{\rm ad}=2/7$, whereas the right panel depicts the scenario for $\mu = 0.5$.
